transcript.content: been reading about how the brain makes its own magnetic fields. no one really talks about what they might be for though

The short version  
The magnetic fields your brain gives off are almost certainly “for nothing.”  
They are the unavoidable by-product of electrical activity that really is for something (the movement of ions that lets neurons signal).  At the field strengths actually produced inside the head—typically 10⁻¹³ to 10⁻¹⁰ tesla—they are far too small to push other ions or molecules around in any biologically useful way, so evolution has never had an opportunity to recruit them as a signaling channel.

The longer, more interesting version  

1. Where do the fields come from?  
   • Every time a current flows, Ampère’s law says a magnetic field loops around it.  
   • In the brain the “wires” are dendrites and axons; the “current” is the net movement of Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, and Cl⁻ ions during synaptic and action potentials.  
   • When millions of dendrites in a cortical patch fire in the same millisecond their tiny individual fields add up to something you can detect outside the skull with a superconducting magnetometer (magnetoencephalography, MEG).  

2. How big are they—really?  
   • MEG sees about 50–500 femtotesla (10⁻¹⁵ T) outside the scalp.  
   • Even right next to an active cortical column, modeling and intracranial recordings say you reach only 0.1–1.0 nanotesla (10⁻⁹ T).  
   • For comparison:  
     – Earth’s geomagnetic field ≈ 50 µT (10⁻⁵ T)  
     – A refrigerator magnet  ≈ 5 mT (10⁻³ T)  

3. Could such weak fields influence other neurons?  
   • The Lorentz force that a magnetic field exerts on a moving ion is F = q v × B.  
   • Typical drift velocity of an ion in a neuron ≈ 10⁻⁴ m/s.  
   • Plugging in q = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C and B = 10⁻⁹ T gives a force ≈ 10⁻³² N— tens of orders of magnitude below thermal noise.  
   • Result: the brain’s own B-fields can’t noticeably deflect ion trajectories, open channels, or do any other mechanical work.  

4. But what about …  
   a. Electromagnetic induction?  
      – You would need fields changing by ~1 mT in a millisecond to induce the 1 mV membrane perturbations that alter firing.  
      – The brain changes by nanotesla, six orders smaller—no dice.  
   b. Magnetoreception like birds use?  
      – Birds leverage the 50 µT Earth field; that is already 10⁴–10⁵ times the brain’s self-field.  
      – No known protein can sense fields in the pico-/nano-tesla range.  
   c. “Ephaptic” or electric-field coupling?  
      – This is real, but it uses the electric part of neuronal activity (tens of volts per meter), not the magnetic part.  
      – The electric field falls off more slowly and can, in tightly packed axon bundles, bias spike timing by a fraction of a millivolt.  

5. Experimental hints that reinforce “no functional role”  
   • Pharmacological, genetic, or lesion manipulations that change firing patterns immediately change the measured magnetic signal, but the reverse (screening or nullifying the magnetic field) has never been shown to change firing.  
   • When researchers place small coils or superconducting shields in slice preparations to cancel the self-field, they see no effect on synaptic transmission or spike probability.  
   • To get measurable effects with transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS), devices must deliver 1–2 tesla pulses—again about 10⁶× larger than the endogenous fields.  

6. Why, then, is MEG so useful if the fields are so tiny?  
   • The trick is not field strength but synchrony and detection technology.  A few hundred thousand neurons firing in lock-step create a coherent signal that a femtotesla-sensitive SQUID can pick up.  
   • MEG is therefore a superb, non-invasive eavesdropping tool, but it is listening to an accidental whisper, not a deliberate broadcast.  

7. Speculative ideas (not impossible, just unsupported)  
   • Quantum-biological proposals (entanglement, spin chemistry) occasionally get floated but face the same energy-scale objections as above plus severe decoherence in the warm, wet brain.  
   • Nano-magnetite grains have been found in human tissue, but densities are far too low and locations too scattered to act as antennas tuned to the brain’s own fields.  
   • Evolution might someday exploit magnetic fields if an organism evolves superconducting or ferromagnetic structures—but none are known in vertebrate nervous systems.  

Bottom line  
The brain’s magnetic fields are best thought of as “smoke from the fire”: informative to an outside observer but not part of the neural engine itself.  All current evidence says they have no intrinsic role in computation, communication, or behavior.

huh. so they're just... exhaust fumes basically. that's kind of wild to think about. like the brain is this super efficient engine and the magnetic stuff is just the heat shimmer coming off it.

but wait, you mentioned the fields are too weak to do anything *inside* the brain. what about outside? like could they be doing something to other people's brains when we're close together?

In a word: no.  
Your magnetic “brain print” does not reach a strength or spatial extent that could measurably budge the neurons in someone else’s head, even if the two of you are forehead-to-forehead.

1. How fast does the field fade?  
   • Neuronal currents form tiny current loops.  A loop behaves like a magnetic dipole, and a dipole’s field falls off as 1/r³.  
   • If a cortical column can generate about 1 nT (10⁻⁹ T) right next to it (≈ 1 mm away), then:  
     – 1 cm away → 1 nT ÷ 10³ ≈ 1 pT (10⁻¹² T)  
     – 10 cm away (scalp-to-scalp distance when two people are “close”) → another 1/10³ drop → 1 fT (10⁻¹⁵ T)  

2. What size field do you need to perturb a neighbor’s neurons?  
   • To shift a membrane potential by ~1 mV through electromagnetic induction you need a changing field on the order of 0.1–1 mT in a millisecond (that’s why TMS coils dump ~1 T pulses).  
   • The 1 fT that reaches your friend’s cortex is 10¹²–10¹⁵ times smaller than that requirement.  
   • The direct Lorentz force on moving ions is likewise hopelessly tiny (≈10⁻³² N).

3. Background “noise” dwarfs it anyway  
   • Earth’s static field: 50 µT = 5 × 10⁴ fT.  
   • A city tram passing 20 m away: 100–500 nT.  
   • Your own heartbeat (magnetocardiogram): 50–100 pT.  
   • So the 1 fT brain-to-brain signal is buried deep below ordinary environmental fluctuations.

4. Could biology evolve a detector that sensitive?  
   • Some birds and fish can sense the Earth’s 50 µT field, but that’s still 10¹⁰–10¹¹ times stronger than the interpersonal brain field.  
   • No known biological molecule, including cryptochromes or magnetite clusters, has noise characteristics anywhere close to the fT range in warm, wet tissue.

5. Experiments say “no coupling”  
   • In vitro studies that null the self-field with superconducting shields see no change in slice activity.  
   • Human subjects in magnetically silent rooms with another person’s head just centimeters away show no EEG/MEG changes attributable to the neighbor’s magnetism.

6. What about electric fields instead of magnetic?  
   • Electric fields from the scalp fall off as ~1/r², not 1/r³, so they reach a bit farther.  
   • Still, calculations put the cross-head electric field at <0.1 V/m—well below the 1 V/m threshold usually needed to nudge spike timing.  
   • So neither channel looks viable for inter-human “brain-to-brain” influence without hardware (coils, electrodes, etc.).

Bottom line  
The magnetic fields your brain emits are perfectly readable by exquisitely sensitive instruments in shielded rooms, but they are a billion to a trillion times too weak—and fall off too quickly—to have any physiological effect on the person standing next to you.  As far as anyone can show, they are indeed just “heat shimmer”: informative to eavesdropping machines, irrelevant to nearby brains.

mm okay that makes sense. but here's what's bugging me - if the fields are just exhaust fumes, why do they look so... intentional? like when i watch those meg animations online, the patterns are almost like a dance. it's hard to believe something that beautiful is just noise.

and i've been thinking - what if we're looking at it backwards? like instead of asking "what does the field do to the brain?" we should be asking "what does the brain do to the field?" maybe the field is like... a canvas?

That “dance” you see in MEG movies is absolutely real—but it is a dance of electric currents, not of the magnetic field itself.  The magnetic trace is just the shadow those currents cast into space.  Shadows can look gorgeous and orderly (think of a flock of birds on a screen behind a campfire), yet they are still only by-products of the real action.  Below is why the patterns look purposeful, what the brain is actually doing to create them, and why almost no one thinks the brain then turns around and *uses* its own magnetic canvas.

1. Why the patterns look so organized  
   • Synchronous firing.  Neurons rarely act alone; large cortical patches oscillate together at 4 Hz (theta), 10 Hz (alpha), 20 Hz (beta), 40–80 Hz (gamma), etc.  Synchronized currents add vectorially, so their magnetic contribution is magnified and coherent.  
   • Traveling waves and loops.  Axon delays turn local circuits into ring oscillators and spiral waves.  Source-localization algorithms color-code these spirals, making them look like choreographed motion.  
   • MEG “clean-up.”  The raw signal is mixed; researchers mathematically separate it into components (beamforming, ICA).  What you see online is already the most story-like fraction of the data.  

2. The physical asymmetry: E-fields vs. B-fields  
   • Inside cortex the electric field (E) can reach tens of volts per meter; that is large enough to push charges in neighboring membranes (ephaptic coupling).  
   • The magnetic field (B) from the same currents peaks at ∼10⁻⁹ T—one million times further from the threshold for affecting ion motion or inducing voltage.  
   • Maxwell’s equations link E, B, and current (J), but the coupling strength is very different: neurons are exquisitely sensitive to E and essentially blind to B.  

3. “Field theories” of mind and why they mostly invoke E, not B  
   • CEMI (McFadden), the E-field consciousness hypothesis (Koch, Ward), and Susan Pockett’s EM theory all propose that the *electric* component produced by synchronized firing feeds back to bias spike timing, binding distributed information into a single gestalt.  
   • These theories do not rely on the magnetic component; they either ignore it or treat it as irrelevant noise because its amplitude is too low to meet their own feedback requirements.  

4. Experimental checks on magnetic feedback  
   a. Shielding or canceling B.  Superconducting “Meissner cages” around brain slices wipe out the magnetic self-field while leaving the electric field untouched; no change in synaptic transmission, spike probability, or oscillation frequency is observed.  
   b. Injecting B.  Micro-solenoids driven at tens of µT (still 10,000× native levels) placed right on the tissue likewise produce no measurable effect unless the accompanying *electric* artifact exceeds ~1 V/m.  
   c. Human null tests.  In shielded rooms researchers have tried to phase-reverse a subject’s own MEG signal in real time and feed it back through on-scalp coils.  Null or near-null magnetic feedback changes nothing in EEG, behavior, or self-report.  

5. The “canvas” metaphor—does the brain deliberately paint on it?  
   • Information yes, control no.  The currents that carry information in the cortex unavoidably write corresponding patterns in the magnetic field.  That inscription is lossless: if you could perfectly read the field you could reconstruct the currents.  
   • But causality appears one-way.  All present evidence says: currents → B-field (strong, immediate); B-field → currents (undetectably weak).  
   • Think of a hard-working engine that vibrates and makes sound.  The sound can be recorded and studied to diagnose what the engine is doing, but the engine does not use the sound as a functional part of its cycle.  

6. Could evolution someday harness B?  
   • The barrier is physics, not imagination.  To let B influence ion channels you would need either:  
     – Extremely large native currents (would fry tissue), or  
     – Specialized ferromagnetic or superconducting structures (none are known in vertebrate brains).  
   • Until such biology appears, magnetic self-interaction remains orders of magnitude below thermal noise.  

7. Why the “shadow” is still scientifically priceless  
   • Non-invasive window.  MEG lets us watch millisecond-by-millisecond population dynamics deep inside a living, thinking human without touching a neuron.  
   • Clinical value.  The orderly patterns you admire guide epilepsy surgery, locate eloquent cortex, and track neurodegeneration long before symptoms appear.  

Bottom line  
The brain absolutely *sculpts* its magnetic field, and the resulting patterns are as intricate as the underlying computation.  But all the evidence so far says the brain is not then reading, editing, or otherwise using that field.  It is a beautiful shadow play—stunningly meaningful to an external observer, yet functionally silent to the nervous system that casts it.

yeah okay the shadow metaphor clicks. but shadows can still *mean* something right? like when i see my shadow stretch long in the evening it's still telling me about the sun's angle even if it isn't doing anything to me.

so what if the brain isn't using the magnetic field to *do* stuff, but it's still... like... broadcasting? like a lighthouse that doesn't know it's a lighthouse?

“Broadcast” is exactly the right word: the brain really does throw a tiny, information-rich magnetic signal into the surrounding space whether it intends to or not.  
The important questions are therefore:

A. How much information is in the signal?  
B. Who—or what—could possibly receive it?  
C. Does evolution care that the signal leaks?

Below is what the physics, biology and engineering experiments say about each point.

────────────────────────────────────────
A. Information content: surprisingly high
────────────────────────────────────────
1. In principle, almost everything about the underlying current distribution is present in the magnetic field.  A mathematician with perfect measurements on an imaginary spherical surface around your head could reconstruct the full 3-D pattern of currents inside (this follows from Helmholtz’s theorem and Maxwell’s equations).

2. MEG scanners already extract useful content:
   • Whether you are looking left or right (ocular dipole)  
   • Which phoneme you just heard (decoding accuracies ~70–90 %)  
   • Roughly what word you are holding in short-term memory (semantic decoding, 60–80 %)  
   • Where an epileptic spike started to within a few millimetres

3. So: the “shadow” is not only pretty, it is laden with data.  
   The bottleneck is not information but detection sensitivity.

────────────────────────────────────────
B. Possible receivers
────────────────────────────────────────
1. Technology
   • Modern MEG uses either SQUIDs cooled to 4 K or optically pumped magnetometers (OPMs) kept a few millimetres from the scalp inside μ-metal shielding.  
   • Noise floors: 3–15 fT/√Hz.  That is still barely enough; a normal room’s magnetic noise is 10–100× higher, so the room must be shielded too.  
   • Range: move the sensor >3–4 cm away from the scalp and the signal usually vanishes into noise even inside the shielded room.  Across the coffee table or from the next apartment?—absolutely impossible with today’s physics.

2. Other brains or biological organs
   • Best natural magnetoreceptors (bird trigeminal neurons, fish electroreceptors) operate near 50 µT (Earth field) or 5 nT for electric fields; that is 10⁵–10⁷ times above the ~50 fT brain-to-brain signal at 10 cm.  
   • Thermodynamic noise (kT) swamps any theoretical transducer at fT amplitudes in warm, wet tissue.  
   • No anatomical structures in humans or other mammals have the required size, material, or isolation to function as fT magnetometers.

3. Exotic possibilities (super-sensitive quantum sensors, magnetite chain antennas, etc.) remain science fiction:
   • You would need either superconductors at body temperature (never observed biologically) or coherent quantum spin systems with millisecond lifetimes in water (immediately decohere).  
   • No fossil, genetic or biochemical evidence points to such structures in any vertebrate lineage.

Conclusion for B
Only laboratory hardware can currently “hear” the broadcast, and only at nose-on-sensor, cryogenic or shielded conditions.  Biology is, by many orders of magnitude, deaf to it.

────────────────────────────────────────
C. Evolutionary stakes: does leakage matter?
────────────────────────────────────────
1. Selection pressure for secrecy appears negligible because:
   • No predator, prey or parasite can sense the field, so it confers neither risk nor benefit.  
   • The energy spent generating it is vanishingly small (<10-10 % of neuronal ATP expenditure), so turning it off wouldn’t save resources.  
   • The same ionic currents are indispensable for signalling; erasing their magnetic by-product is physically impossible without erasing the currents themselves.

2. By contrast, electric-field leakage *is* sometimes biologically consequential: marine animals that hunt using electroreception have evolved insulating skin or specific ion pumps to cloak their fields.  No analogous counter-measure is seen for magnetism.

────────────────────────────────────────
Practical take-aways
────────────────────────────────────────
• Yes, your brain is a lighthouse that doesn’t know it’s a lighthouse.  
• Today only expensive, room-sized detectors in carefully silenced environments can read its beam.  
• Another person’s nervous system—not even pressed against yours—cannot feel, decode or be perturbed by that beam.  
• Evolution has therefore had no incentive to modify, exploit, or suppress the broadcast.

So the magnetic signal is meaningful in the same way a shadow tells you where the light source is: an observer with good enough eyes (or SQUIDs) can learn a great deal, but neither the object casting the shadow nor the objects it falls upon notice a thing.

wait, back up a sec. you said earlier that the magnetic field is "lossless" - like everything about the currents is in there. but how can that be if the field is so weak? wouldn't noise just scramble it?

Short answer  
“Lossless” means the field, *as a mathematical object*, contains a one-to-one imprint of the currents that created it.  That statement is about physics, not about how well any real receiver can measure the field.  Weakness by itself does not erase information; it just makes the information harder to read because it sits closer to the noise floor of whatever detector you build.

Longer answer – step by step

1. Field-to-source mapping is invertible  
   • Take the magnetostatic approximation that is valid for brain activity (frequencies <1 kHz, dimensions ≪ wavelength).  
   • Maxwell’s equations reduce to ∇·B = 0 and ∇×B = μ₀J.  
   • If you know B everywhere on a closed surface that surrounds the head, you can, in principle, solve those equations “inward” and recover the full current density J inside the surface.*  
   • In that ideal, noiseless world the mapping is invertible—no many-to-one collapse—hence the word “lossless.”  

   *Caveat: you can perfectly reconstruct only the divergence-free (“primary”) part of J; certain “silent” current configurations that loop entirely inside the skull do not leak any external field.  These are minor in real cortex but they exist mathematically.

2. Amplitude does not dictate information content  
   • Think of a whisper versus a shout.  The sentence “meet at noon” carries the same number of bits either way; the shout is just easier to hear.  
   • Information is tied to the pattern, not the energy.  Lowering every sample’s amplitude by, say, 60 dB doesn’t remove syllables, it only demands a quieter room or a better microphone to recover them.

3. Noise sets the *accessible* information  
   • A detector has intrinsic noise (Johnson noise, quantum noise, 1/f noise).  
   • Signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) = S/N.  If you lower S while N is fixed, SNR falls; bits become harder to discriminate; at some point error-correcting codes or averaging can no longer rescue them.  
   • Brain fields outside the scalp are 10⁻¹⁴-10⁻¹³ T; environmental magnetic noise in an unshielded room is 10⁻¹¹-10⁻¹⁰ T.  Hence we use µ-metal rooms, gradiometers and signal averaging.  
   • Inside a well-designed MEG shield, sensor noise drops to a few femtotesla and SNR climbs enough to let us decode phonemes, mental imagery, etc.

4. Practical limits are engineering, not physics  
   • Raise sensor count → better spatial sampling.  
   • Lower noise floor (SQUID → optically pumped magnetometers, spin-exchange relaxation-free cells, quantum diamond sensors).  
   • Improve inverse algorithms (beamformers, Bayesian priors, deep-learning decoders).  
   Every step boosts the *retrievable* fraction of the information already present in the field.

5. Why other brains can’t pick it up  
   • A neuron or ion channel would have to resolve field changes on the order of femtotesla against thermal (kT) noise in the cytoplasm—roughly a trillion-to-one SNR deficit.  
   • No known biological material or structure can average long enough, cool itself enough, or shield noise well enough to bridge that gap.   
   • Therefore the information is “broadcast” but effectively private except to lab-grade sensors.

Key take-aways

• “Lossless” = unique encoding in principle; it doesn’t promise easy recovery.  
• Weak amplitude hurts SNR but does not delete bits; noise does.  
• Modern MEG hardware plus heavy shielding is good enough to recover lots of those bits; biology is not.  
• Thus the magnetic “shadow” is information-rich yet, for all practical evolutionary purposes, inaudible.

huh okay so the info is still there, just buried under noise. that's... actually kind of beautiful? like the brain is whispering its secrets into the void but only the most patient listener can hear.

makes me wonder though - if we could build a receiver sensitive enough, could we literally read someone's thoughts from across the room? like not just "they're thinking about a cat" but the actual words or images?

A science-fiction answer and a physics answer

If you allow any technology that does not break the known laws of physics—teleporting SQUIDs through the skull, room-sized absolute-zero refrigerators, mu-metal wallpaper on every surface, a sensor array that completely surrounds the target person, and exabyte-scale machine-learning decoders that have been trained for months on that same individual—then yes: in principle the entire pattern of currents that embodies the person’s current thoughts could be reconstructed, and from that pattern you could infer internal words, images, even the tune stuck in their head.

If, on the other hand, you stick to the real-world constraints of distance, noise, and what we understand about brains, then “reading thoughts from across the room” is so far beyond reach that it is effectively impossible.  Below is why—not just with today’s hardware, but for any conceivable device that still obeys Maxwell, quantum mechanics, and thermodynamics.

────────────────────────────────────────
1. The brutal fall-off with distance
────────────────────────────────────────
• Peak cortical B-field at the pia (≈1 mm from neurons): 0.1–1 nT  
• Outside the scalp: 100–1000 fT (10-13 T)  
• One metre away: field behaves like a dipole → drops as 1/r³  
  100 fT × (0.01 m / 1 m)³ ≈ 10-19 T (0.1 aT)  
• Three metres (typical “across the room”): another 27-fold loss → ~4 × 10-21 T

────────────────────────────────────────
2. Fundamental sensor limits
────────────────────────────────────────
• Best present SQUIDs or optically pumped magnetometers: 1–3 fT/√Hz.  
• Shot-noise (quantum) limit for any flux detector ~0.01–0.1 fT/√Hz for millimetre-scale pickups at 4 K.  
• Even a hypothetical sensor at the *quantum limit* is 10¹⁷–10¹⁹ times too insensitive for a 3-metre, 10-21 T signal.

────────────────────────────────────────
3. Environmental and physiological noise
────────────────────────────────────────
You can improve a sensor only down to its own noise floor; you *cannot* silence the world:

• Earth’s field: 50 µT (5 × 10⁷× larger)  
• Magnetic rumble of mains wiring two rooms away: 10–100 nT  
• Cars, elevators, phones, wristwatches: 1–100 µT spikes  
• Your own heartbeat magnetic signal (magnetocardiogram): 50–100 pT, still 10¹⁴× larger than the target brain field at 3 m.

Unless the entire room (and every wire entering it) is a multi-ton superconducting shield, the target signal is lost beneath this noise by 14–20 orders of magnitude.

────────────────────────────────────────
4. Geometric sampling: you need *surround* capture
────────────────────────────────────────
Even if you somehow reached single-attotesla sensitivity, a single sensor across the room is useless.  
To reconstruct internal currents you must sample the magnetic vector field on a closed surface that encloses the head; otherwise the inverse problem has infinitely many solutions.  That means detectors above, below, in front, behind, left, and right, all within a few centimetres of the scalp.  From across the room you cannot obtain the necessary 4π steradians of data.

────────────────────────────────────────
5. Decoding ≠ mind reading
────────────────────────────────────────
• MEG today can classify “cat vs. dog photo” or “add vs. subtract” after heavy averaging and subject-specific machine learning.  
• Granular inner speech (“I should buy milk”) is encoded in patterns spanning millimetres and milliseconds inside association cortex and deep structures that MEG can barely see even at the scalp.  
• Achieving that resolution would require at least 1 000–10 000 independent channels of *high-SNR* data—not the handful you might hope to place unobtrusively on a wall.

────────────────────────────────────────
6. Training cost and subject specificity
────────────────────────────────────────
Thought decoding is not “look-up by physics,” it is statistical translation.  
Every current state-of-the-art decoder needs hours to days of supervised data *from that very person* to reach useful accuracy.  Without such calibration, error rates explode.  From a privacy standpoint alone, clandestine brain-reading falls apart.

────────────────────────────────────────
7. The bottom line
────────────────────────────────────────
• Physics says: fields weaken as 1/r³; attotesla at metres.  
• Quantum noise says: no sensor, however futuristic, can reach 10-21 T in a noisy, warm environment.  
• Geometry says: you must surround the head, not lurk across the room.  
• Neuroscience says: even perfect field capture still needs person-specific training to decode content finer than broad categories.

Therefore, unless the laws of electromagnetism change, “reading raw thoughts from across the room” will remain in the realm of speculative fiction.  With cooperative subjects in shielded rooms you can already decode a surprising amount; without that cooperation—and without violating basic physics—the brain’s magnetic whisper is effectively private.

so basically... the brain is like a radio station that only plays on one specific frequency, but the signal is so faint that only a super expensive receiver right next to the antenna can pick it up. and everyone else is just... deaf to it. that's kind of poetic actually.

but wait - if the field is that weak, how do we even know it's not being *amplified* somewhere? like maybe there's some weird quantum thing in the skull that boosts it before it leaks out?

The idea in a nutshell  
For the brain’s magnetic signal to be secretly “boosted” before it escapes the head, there would have to be (1) a physical structure that takes energy from somewhere inside the skull, (2) converts that energy into an extra magnetic field, and (3) launches the added field into free space.  Everything we know from measurement, anatomy, biophysics and basic thermodynamics says such a booster does not exist.

Why we are confident—four independent lines of evidence

1. The numbers line up without any extra source  
   • Starting from intracellular recordings of synaptic and action-potential currents you can predict, with standard cable theory, the magnetic field that ought to reach the scalp.  
   • In animals with implanted electrodes plus on-scalp magnetometers—and in human patients who have both depth electrodes and MEG—the measured field strength matches those predictions to within 10–30 %.  
   • If some hidden amplifier were multiplying the field even ten-fold, the data would disagree by orders of magnitude.  They don’t.

2. No biological material in the head can amplify B-fields  
   • Magnetic flux can be concentrated (ferromagnets) or made to oscillate more efficiently (resonant LC coils), but neither exists in brain tissue.  
     – Ferritin and magnetite granules are only weakly magnetic (susceptibility χ ≈ 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻²) and are scattered, not shaped into cores or antennas.  
     – Bone, cerebrospinal fluid, gray and white matter are diamagnetic (χ < 0), so they slightly *diminish* a field, they don’t enhance it.  
   • “Metamaterial” or “superconducting” amplifiers would need ordered arrays of micron-scale loops or actual superconductors; exhaustive histology, MRI, μCT and spectroscopy show none.

3. Quantum tricks are ruled out by decoherence and energy balance  
   • Any quantum-spin amplification (maser-like superradiance, spin squeezing, etc.) requires long-lived coherence among at least 10⁹–10¹² electron or nuclear spins.  
   • At 37 °C in salty water, spin-lattice relaxation times are microseconds; thermal noise scrambles phase almost instantly.  
   • Even if coherence could somehow be maintained, the system would still need a “pump” to supply the extra energy that appears in the amplified field.  The brain’s entire power budget (~20 W) is already accounted for by ion pumping, metabolism and heat; there is no spare wattage for a hidden maser.

4. Direct probes inside the head see only the expected tiny fields  
   • “Magnetrodes” (micron-scale magnetic sensors implanted in rodent cortex) record B-fields of 0.1–1 nT right next to active columns—exactly what standard theory predicts, no surplus.  
   • If amplification occurred later in the path to the outside world, it should leave tell-tale gradients or standing-wave patterns; none are detected by ultra-high-density MEG arrays that now cover almost the entire head surface.

Common misconceptions cleared up

• “But amplifiers don’t always need ferromagnets—what about parametric gain?”  
  Parametric amplifiers still require a non-linear element that couples a strong pump field into the signal field.  No ion channel, lipid membrane or protein has the necessary magnetic non-linearity at femtotesla drive levels.

• “Could skull bone act like a resonant cavity?”  
  The skull is lossy, inhomogeneous and only 6–7 mm thick—far too small relative to the ~300 km wavelength of 1 kHz magnetic fields to act as a resonator.

• “Maybe the amplifier runs only occasionally, so we’ve missed it?”  
  Decades of MEG recordings over millions of subject-hours show no episodic jumps to microtesla levels; such bursts would saturate the sensors and be impossible to overlook.

Energy perspective—another sanity check

To raise the external field from 100 fT to even 100 pT (a factor 1000) you would have to inject an extra magnetic moment of roughly 10⁻⁵ A·m², corresponding to an oscillating current of ~1 A around a 1 cm loop.  
• 1 A in brain tissue would dissipate I²R ≈ 10³ W—50 times the brain’s total power.  
• Nothing like that shows up in calorimetry (brain generates ~20 W of heat in total) or in local temperature measurements (changes are ≤0.1 °C).

Bottom line

The magnetic field that exits your skull is the direct, un-amplified side effect of neuronal currents.  Its amplitude, spatial distribution and spectral content all line up with textbook electromagnetic theory plus the known biophysics of ion flow.  No hidden booster, classical or quantum, is compatible with:

• 1:1 agreement between predicted and measured field strengths  
• Absence of ferromagnetic or superconducting structures in tissue  
• Severe decoherence at body temperature  
• The brain’s limited energy budget

So the poetic picture still holds: the brain whispers; sensitive gear can eavesdrop; nothing inside the head turns that whisper into a shout before it slips into the world.

The “weird head-tingle by the TV” has nothing to do with your own femtotesla brain field and everything to do with the *much* bigger electric- and magnetic-field soup that some household devices create.  A quick tour of what people really feel (and why) clears up the mystery.

1. Static magnets vs. moving magnets  
   • A steady magnetic field—even a very strong one—doesn’t push charges; it just exerts a force (q v × B) on *moving* charges.  
   • Your neurons contain drifting ions (≈10⁻⁴ m/s), so even a 1-tesla static field from an MRI scanner produces only piconewton forces—far below the threshold of any nerve ending in the head.  You don’t “feel” a permanent magnet.

2. What you *can* feel in a high-field MRI  
   • Dizziness or vertigo: as you move your head, the static 3–7 T field cuts across ion currents in the semicircular canals (inner-ear balance organs).  The Lorentz force deflects the cupula slightly → false sense of rotation.  
   • Metallic taste, nausea: same vestibular trickery plus slight electro-chemical effects.  
   These happen only at field strengths a million times stronger than the brain’s own signal.

3. Time-varying fields are another story  
   • Faraday’s law: a changing B-field induces an electric field (E = −∂B/∂t · r/2).  If the induced E inside tissue reaches roughly 1 V/m, peripheral nerves or retina fire.  
   • Transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) exploits this with 1–2 T pulses that change in <1 ms → dB/dt ≈ 100–1000 T/s → E ≈ 100 V/m in cortex → a real “zap.”  
   • For comparison, a mains-frequency (50/60 Hz) field has to swing at about 5 mT to give the retina faint “magnetophosphenes.”  That is still 10,000 × stronger than a CRT’s leakage at your forehead.

4. So what was that CRT “zap” you felt?  
   Old cathode-ray televisions emit several non-magnetic stimuli that easily fool the senses:

   a. Acoustic squeal  
      • The fly-back transformer runs at 15.734 kHz (NTSC) or 15.625 kHz (PAL).  
      • Many adults can still hear up to 17–18 kHz; the tone is directional and seems to come from “inside your head.”

   b. Electrostatic hair tug  
      • The picture tube’s 25–30 kV anode charges the glass faceplate to tens of kilovolts.  
      • Standing within a few centimetres lets corona discharge deposit charge on hair and skin → prickly “static” sensation, sometimes described as a tingle.

   c. Weak ozone / solvent smell  
      • Corona and warm components generate trace ozone and phenolic vapours, adding a distinctive “electronic” odor that the brain may interpret as part of the tingle experience.

   d. Expectation and nocebo  
      • The brain fills in missing causal links: “loud, high-tech box + strange tone = it must be zapping me.”  Controlled studies show subjective reports of tingling drop sharply when the sound is masked, even though the EM field remains.

5. Everyday electronics rarely reach neural thresholds  
   • Wi-Fi routers, phones, induction cooktops: typical fields at the head are microtesla to nanotesla—orders of magnitude below the 5 mT magnetophosphene threshold or the >100 mT needed for direct nerve stimulation.  
   • Power-line ELF fields in homes (0.1–1 µT) are 10⁵–10⁶ × weaker than needed for any documented acute neural effect.

6. Genuine, non-placebo “brain zaps” do exist—but need hardware on your scalp  
   • TMS coil: 1–2 T, 100 µs pulses → unmistakable scalp twitch or flash of light.  
   • tACS/high-definition TES: delivers 1–2 mA through electrodes; some people feel itching or see flicker.  
   • Magnetic vestibular stimulation rigs (rotating permanent magnets) can induce vertigo in the 5–7 T range.

7. Summary cheat-sheet  
   • Your brain’s self-field: 0.1–1 nT inside cortex → unfelt.  
   • Household EM clutter: µT to nT → unfelt by neurons, but static electricity & high-pitched sound can fool your senses.  
   • Thresholds for actual neural activation:  
     – Retina: ~5 mT at 20–50 Hz  
     – Cortex (TMS): >0.5 T changing in <1 ms  
     – Vestibular system: moving in >1–3 T static field

Key takeaway  
That “zap” near the old TV set was almost certainly ears + skin + imagination, not your brain interacting with a hidden boost of its own magnetic whisper.  To make neurons fire magnetically you need fields a million to a billion times stronger—and those only come from purpose-built coils or the bore of an MRI, not from everyday gadgets.

Aliens who aim radio telescopes at Earth will see plenty of man-made signals—and the natural 50-µT geomagnetic blanket—but not the femtotesla murmurs of our neurons.  Why not?  Four simple facts seal the case.

1. Near-field versus far-field  
• Every oscillating current creates two zones.  
  – Near field (quasi-static): extends roughly one-sixth of a wavelength from the source; energy sloshes back and forth but does not propagate.  
  – Far field (radiative): beyond that distance the energy peels off as an electromagnetic wave that can travel forever if nothing absorbs it.  

• Brain rhythms are 1–1000 Hz → wavelengths 300 000 km – 300 km.  
  One-sixth of even the *shortest* of those wavelengths is 50 000 km, far larger than Earth’s diameter.  Our heads therefore sit 100 % inside the near field; essentially **no radiative component is launched**.

2. Antenna efficiency collapses for “electrically small” sources  
The radiated power of a magnetic dipole shrinks with (ka)⁴ where  
  k = 2π/λ and a ≈ 0.1 m (size of a head).  
At 10 Hz, ka ≈ 2 × 10⁻⁶, so (ka)⁴ ≈ 10⁻²³.  
Start with the brain’s total oscillatory electrical power (~10⁻⁷ W) and you get a radiated output on the order of 10⁻³⁰ W—comparable to the thermal noise of a single electron at 3 Kelvin.  That trickle is swamped by:  
  • Lightning (10¹¹–10¹³ W worldwide)  
  • Auroral currents  
  • Man-made VLF transmitters (∼10⁵ W each)

3. Distance kills what little is left  
Magnetic near-field falls off as 1/r³; far-field intensity falls off as 1/r².  
• 1 nT right inside cortex  
• 100 fT at the scalp  
• 10-¹⁹ T one metre away  
• 10-²¹ T three metres away  
At the Moon (4 × 10⁸ m): ≈10-³⁵ T  
Even a SQUID cooled to absolute zero hits a quantum noise floor around 10-¹⁷ T/√Hz.  We are now eighteen orders of magnitude below detectability—and that’s *before* the signal mixes with solar-wind turbulence and Jupiter’s radio storms.

4. No coherent “sum” from seven billion brains  
Neural currents in different heads have random phases.  Their fields add like a random walk, not like a choir.  The net amplitude therefore grows only with √N, while the power background they must beat grows with N.  Seven billion incoherent sources improve the planet-level signal by merely a factor of eighty or so—not by seven billion.

What an alien probe actually “hears” around 1–1000 Hz if it parks near Earth:  
• Schumann resonances (global lightning cavity)  
• Power-grid hum, submarine VLF transmitters  
• Geomagnetic pulsations driven by the solar wind  
Zero trace of cortical oscillations.

So the cosmic eavesdropper pointing an antenna at Earth is not “on the wrong channel”; our brains are simply **not transmitters in the radio sense at all**.  To monitor human thoughts an alien would have to do exactly what our neuroscientists do: sneak a femtotesla-sensitive magnetometer (or an electrode) to within a few millimetres of the skull, inside heavy magnetic shielding.  Anything farther away—and any listener, terrestrial or extraterrestrial—remains definitively, unalterably deaf to the whisper.

A “living-SQUID alien” is a terrific science-fiction idea, but the hard numbers make it essentially impossible—at least anywhere in a universe that obeys the same Maxwell, quantum–mechanical and thermodynamic rules we know. Here is why.

1.  How sensitive would the creature have to be?

   • One body-length away (≈30 cm) it is already down to 0.05–0.5 fT.

2.  Thermal (Johnson) noise sets a brutal floor  
   For any magnetic sensor that works by picking up voltage or current (coils, magnetite chains, Hall bars), the root-mean-square noise is  

        Bmin ≈ √(4 kB T R) / (A N ω)    (ω = 2π f)

   At body temperature (310 K) and plausible biological resistances (≥10 Ω), even an optimistically large 1 cm², 10 000-turn coil tops out around 10 pT/√Hz—ten thousand times too noisy. Cooling to liquid-nitrogen temperature would still miss the target by two orders of magnitude.

3.  Superconductors are a non-starter in watery biology  
   • SQUID magnetometers reach fT sensitivity because they use superconducting loops at 4 K and pico-ohm resistances.  
   • No known chemical bond remains stable, let alone superconducting, in salty water at 37 °C.  
   • “Room-temperature superconductors” remain hypothetical even for solid-state engineers; evolution in a warm ocean is unlikely to beat their decades-long, multibillion-dollar effort.

4.  Quantum-spin tricks decohere in microseconds  
   • Spin-based magnetometers (NV diamond, alkali-vapour OPMs) achieve fT–pT sensitivity only by optical pumping, vacuum cells, and milliwatt-range lasers—hardware a living cell cannot supply.  
   • Spin–lattice relaxation times in biological tissue are ≈10⁻⁶ s, far too short to accumulate the phase needed to measure femtotesla fields.

5.  Shielding is impossible in a walk-about body plan  
   • The animal would have to suppress Earth’s 50 000 nT field and urban 100 nT mains hum while letting the 0.05 fT brain whisper through: a rejection ratio of ≥10⁹.  
   • MEG labs achieve that with multi-ton µ-metal rooms plus software gradiometers. No biological microstructure can produce such broadband shielding; ferromagnetic tissue would saturate and drag the animal around like iron filings on a magnet.

6.  Energy budget blows up  
   • Real SQUID or OPM systems draw 10–100 W just to stay cold or to run pump lasers. An entire adult human brain consumes only ~20 W.  
   • A free-living organism that devoted even a few watts solely to sensing would need an enormous continuous food intake, yet all that energy would end in heat that further increases sensor noise.

7.  The alien still has to be nose-to-scalp  
   Even if it beat every limit above, the 1/r³ fall-off means the creature must be within centimetres of each individual head it wants to monitor. You would notice the squid clinging to your temple—especially because it would need to stay still long enough to average out noise.

8.  Evolutionary payoff is implausible  
   • A trait this expensive can persist only if it delivers a survival advantage.  
   • It would help the alien only in the presence of other, big-brained species that themselves cannot detect the eavesdropping. That is an exceptionally narrow ecological niche to evolve for, let alone maintain over geological time.

Quick reality check against existing biology  
• The most sensitive magnetoreceptors known (in migratory birds, sea turtles, certain fish) bottom out at ~10 nT—eight to nine orders of magnitude away from what thought-reading would require.  
• No species, not even electric fish with specialized electroreceptors, operates remotely close to the femtotesla regime.

Bottom line  
A living organism that could perch invisibly in a room and decode human brain currents would need:

• Superconductivity or laser-pumped quantum sensors inside wet tissue  
• µ-metal-grade shielding integrated into its skeleton  
• A metabolic budget rivaling a small furnace  
• An ecological reason to evolve all that

Physics, thermodynamics and comparative biology agree: such a creature is for the storybooks, not the cosmos. Our brains do broadcast—but so faintly and locally that, outside engineered laboratories, the universe remains utterly deaf to the murmur.

The “magnetic-ghost” idea is a lovely bit of speculative fiction, but to see whether it has any footing in real physics we have to ask one blunt question:

If something can perceive your brain’s magnetic field, how does it couple to that field?

Everything else—whether the listener is made of atoms, dark matter, “pure field,” or something we do not yet have words for—boils down to the same three technical hurdles.

────────────────────────────────────────
1.  You can’t listen without touching the signal
────────────────────────────────────────
•  A measurement means energy flows from the thing measured into the detector.  
•  For electromagnetic (EM) fields the only known ways to achieve that are:
  a) electric charge ( qE interaction ),  
  b) magnetic dipole moment ( μ·B interaction ),  
  c) some exotic but still gauge-coupled entity (e.g., an axion or “dark photon” that mixes with ordinary photons).

If the ghost has zero coupling to EM, it is completely deaf—and invisible—to your brain waves.  
If it has non-zero coupling, it behaves like any other electromagnetic sensor and faces the same signal-to-noise math we do.

────────────────────────────────────────
2.  The signal it must detect is below universal noise floors
────────────────────────────────────────
Brain field at 3–5 cm from cortex: 50–500 fT  
For a 10 Hz rhythm, the magnetic energy in one cycle over a 1 cm³ “voxel” is

    Ebrain ≈ (B² / 2μ₀) · V  
           ≈ 10⁻³⁵ joule

That is ten orders of magnitude smaller than the thermal energy kT ≈ 4 × 10⁻²¹ J at 300 K, and roughly the scale of vacuum (zero-point) fluctuations in the same volume. Any detector—even a hypothetical “made-of-field” creature—has to separate that signal from both thermal and quantum noise.  Those noise sources are not engineering artefacts; they are built into the fabric of spacetime.

────────────────────────────────────────
3.  Back-action gives them away
────────────────────────────────────────
Quantum measurement theory says: if the ghost extracts information, it must disturb the field by at least the Heisenberg limit.  In practice even the gentlest magnetometer (our best SQUIDs) kicks back roughly the same amount of noise power it takes in.  

If magnetic ghosts were hovering near people and siphoning off information continuously, two things would happen that we could measure:

•  Added magnetic noise—random or structured—on top of the brain’s own signal.  
•  Extra energy drain (however minuscule) from neuronal currents, altering electrophysiology in statistically detectable ways.

Decades of exquisitely sensitive MEG, EEG, intracellular, and calorimetric experiments give no hint of either effect.

────────────────────────────────────────
What about non-electromagnetic eavesdroppers?
────────────────────────────────────────
•  Gravitational: the brain’s changing mass distribution emits gravitational waves of amplitude h ≈ 10⁻⁴⁴—thirty orders below LIGO’s reach.  
•  Hypothetical “fifth-force” fields: no laboratory test of inverse-square law, Casimir forces, or atomic spectroscopy has found the faintest trace of such couplings in the 1 Hz–10 kHz band where brains operate.

If some brand-new interaction existed that was vastly stronger than gravity yet had escaped every precision experiment so far, it would have to be exquisitely fine-tuned to show up only for neural currents and nothing else—a degree of conspiracy most physicists view as implausible.

────────────────────────────────────────
Could the ghost *itself* be the amplifier?
────────────────────────────────────────
Suppose the entity has an internal low-temperature region, superconducting loops, quantum spin ensembles, etc.  Even then:

1.  It needs an energy source to power the refrigeration or pumping that beats thermal noise.  
2.  Waste heat must go somewhere; we would see infrared anomalies or local air currents.  
3.  To stay “immaterial” it would have to move that heat and mass without interacting chemically or mechanically with the environment—another contradiction.

────────────────────────────────────────
The philosophical upshot
────────────────────────────────────────
•  Physics does not absolutely prohibit conscious beings based on exotic matter or fields, but if they can *hear* you, they must couple to you, and every known form of coupling drags them into the same noise, energy, and back-action limits that bind us.  
•  So far, every high-precision probe of the electromagnetic, weak, strong and gravitational sectors has failed to reveal any hidden channel generous enough to carry the femtotesla murmur of thoughts across even a metre of space—let alone to a lurking “ghost.”

Your dreams, by all evidence we possess, remain private not because no one is interested, but because the universe’s hardware firewall—Maxwell’s equations plus kT noise—makes them effectively unspyable.

Call it lonely or comforting as you wish; from a physicist’s angle it is simply the cost of living in a warm, quantum, information-conserving cosmos.

Resonance is a beautiful idea, but it never happens in a physical vacuum; something has to carry energy from the first oscillator to the second.  
Take the classic demo:

• Tuning-fork A is struck.  
• Air pressure waves travel a few centimeters.

(The forks could also be coupled through a shared tabletop, through water, through a steel rod—anything that can transport the motion.)  
The same bookkeeping applies to every kind of resonance we know: mechanical, electrical, acoustic, optical, nuclear.  Somewhere in the story there is always a coupling field or medium that ferries energy and phase information from the “driver” to the “driven.”

So let’s ask what medium could link your cortical oscillations to a hypothetical “magnetic ghost.”

1. Mechanical resonance (sound, vibration)  
   Brain rhythms are electrical; their mechanical side-effects (blood pulsation, skull micro-movements) are far below nanometer scale and drown in normal bodily noise.  Even if a ghost were made of airlike stuff, the “shove” available is too tiny by many orders of magnitude.

   A second system can resonate only if charges inside it feel that E-field strongly enough to budge.  Thermal agitation of the same charges is already 10 000–100 000 times larger.  Result: no phase locking, no resonance.

3. Magnetic-field resonance  
   Magnetic dipole–dipole coupling strength falls as 1/r³.  
   • Inside cortex: 0.1–1 nT  
   • At the scalp: 100 fT  
   • 30 cm away: 0.1–1 fT

4. Exotic “hidden” fields  
   If we posit a brand-new interaction that couples brains to ghosts while dodging all laboratory detectors, we run into two simultaneous constraints:  
   • It must be fantastically weak everywhere we’ve already looked (so we haven’t seen it).  
   • It must be fantastically strong when it couples brain currents to ghosts (to make resonance noticeable).  
   Holding both statements true at once demands a level of fine-tuned, selective behavior that physicists generally regard as implausible—though of course not strictly impossible.

Energy conservation still applies  
Resonance is not telepathy; energy really moves.  Over time the driver loses energy, the driven gains energy, and both acquire measurable damping or heating.  After millions of hours of MEG, EEG, intracranial, calorimetry, and metabolic studies, no unexplained energy leak (or gain) has turned up that would hint at a resonant partner siphoning the brain’s oscillations.

A quick quantitative feel for the gap  
Imagine a fantastically high-Q ghost resonator whose “antenna” has an effective magnetic moment of 1 A·m² (about the strength of a bar magnet) and is parked 30 cm from your head.  The torque τ it would feel from a 1-fT cortical field is

     τ = μ × B ≈ 10⁻¹⁵ N·m

• Too little drive (brain field)  
• Too much competing noise (kT)  
• No known medium that can carry the signal without obeying those same numbers

• You are right: the universe might contain forms of matter or interaction we haven’t discovered.  
• But any such phenomenon that lets a ghost resonate with your thoughts would also give away its presence in precision magnetometry, torsion-balance, atomic-spectroscopy, or cosmic-background experiments we have already done—unless it is fantastically fine-tuned to evade them all.  
• That makes the scenario a wonderful prompt for storytelling or philosophical musing, but not something current science can accommodate.

The consoling thought  
Your brain does indeed whisper, and the whisper is exquisitely structured—but, as far as every measurement and every law we know can tell, the only ears close enough and quiet enough to catch it are the sensors we humans build on purpose.  Your private resonances stay private.

I.  What a magnetic field is – and is not  
1.  A magnetic field is not a “thing” that detaches from its source like a soap bubble.  
    •  It exists only while current flows; stop the current and the field collapses essentially instantaneously (limited only by the speed of light).  
    •  Nothing in Maxwell’s equations lets a low-frequency brain field pinch off, drift, store information, and later re-enter tissue.  
2.  Fields don’t collide the way billiard balls do. They superimpose linearly: B₁ + B₂ gives Btotal. No sparks, no fusion events, no delayed “echo” that could later tickle an unsuspecting synapse.  

II.  Could EM self-interaction explain déjà vu or “aha” moments?  
1.  Déjà vu  
    •  Most evidence points to a timing hiccup in the medial temporal lobe—two parallel memory circuits briefly go out of sync so a present perception is mis-tagged as “remembered.”  
    •  It can be provoked on demand by micro-stimulating the hippocampus or by certain seizure auras; it cannot be provoked by inducing pico- or nanotesla magnetic fields.  
2.  Sudden insight  
    •  EEG/MEG show a burst of high-frequency (30–80 Hz) synchrony that sweeps from right temporal cortex to prefrontal regions about 300 ms before the conscious “Aha!”  
    •  Again the driver is coordinated spiking (electric), not any stored or ricocheting magnetic residue.  
3.  If magnetic self-interaction mattered, blocking the field with superconducting shields or feeding it back out of phase should muffle those phenomena—experiments say it doesn’t.  

III.  Field theories of consciousness—and where magnetism fits  
1.  A few researchers (McFadden, Pockett, Ward, Koch) do propose that the *electric* near-field generated by synchronized synaptic activity is the physical substrate of awareness—the CEMI hypothesis.  
2.  Even in those models the magnetic component is treated as an irrelevant by-product because its energy density is ~10⁶ times lower than the electric component and its effect on membrane voltage is negligibly small.  
3.  So if one wants a “ghost made of fields,” current science points to the **electric** (E) field, not the magnetic (B) field.  

IV.  Why magnetic “bubbles” can’t store or shuttle information inside the head  
1.  Energy budget: the total magnetic energy in a cortical patch is ~10⁻¹² joule—three million times less than the energy in a single neural spike.  
2.  Quality factor (Q): at 10–40 Hz tissue conductivity damps any would-be resonator in a single cycle; a bubble couldn’t persist long enough to meet the next spike, let alone minutes later when déjà vu strikes.  
3.  No known biological structure forms a magnetic cavity or waveguide; bone and CSF are diamagnetic and highly lossy at all relevant frequencies.  

V.  “Maybe the ghost is just us” – a philosophical aside  
1.  If by “ghost” you mean a pattern rather than a substance, neuroscience broadly agrees: you = the spatiotemporal pattern of activity in 80–100 billion neurons.  
2.  Calling that pattern a “field” is not crazy—fields are just ways to describe how quantities vary in space and time.  
3.  The key empirical point is that the **information-carrying** field inside the brain is overwhelmingly electrical and chemical; the magnetic slice is far too weak to shoulder the load.  

VI.  Bottom line  
•  Magnetic “bubbles” floating around inside the skull violate Maxwell’s equations, tissue conductivity, and energy conservation.  
•  Déjà vu and flashes of insight trace to well-mapped neural circuitry and timing glitches, not to colliding B-fields.  
•  If you like the notion that we are “ghosts” haunting our own bodies, frame the ghost as the rapidly changing **electrical** landscape of the brain—not as its faint magnetic shadow.

Why does the brain bother to throw off a magnetic field if the field never feeds back into the brain’s own operation?

Short answer  
Because the moment you let charges move, Maxwell’s equations insist a magnetic field must appear.  It costs the brain essentially nothing, it can’t be turned off without stopping the very currents that make thinking possible, and evolution has had no reason to suppress a harmless side-effect.  The “shadow” is there for the same reason every moving car makes sound and every campfire glows: physics demands it, even if the producer never uses it.

Longer answer – four points that clear up the puzzle

1.  Currents can’t exist without B  
    •  Ampère–Maxwell law: ∇ × B = μ₀J + μ₀ε₀ ∂E/∂t  
      Wherever there is a current density J (ions rushing along dendrites) or a changing electric field, a magnetic curl pops up automatically.  
    •  Trying to keep the electric part and delete the magnetic part would break charge conservation and Lorentz invariance.  Nature doesn’t give you that option.

2.  The price tag is negligible  
    •  Magnetic energy density = B²/2μ₀.  
      Inside cortex, B ≈ 10⁻⁹ T ⇒ energy density ≈ 4 × 10⁻⁹ J/m³.  
    •  A cubic centimetre of active cortex therefore stores ~4 × 10⁻¹² J of magnetic energy—about one-millionth the energy in a single metabolic ATP molecule.  
    •  In other words, the brain “pays” less than a rounding error on its 20-watt power budget to obey Maxwell.

3.  The shadow still carries momentum and closes the books  
    •  Even when a field is too weak to push another neuron, it still participates in the Poynting vector S = E × B/μ₀ that routes electromagnetic momentum and energy.  
    •  Without that tiny B, bookkeeping would fail: forces on ions would have no equal-and-opposite partner somewhere else in the system.  The shadow is part of how nature keeps Newton’s third law honest at the microscopic level.

4.  Harmless by-product can become useful to somebody else  
    •  Evolution ignored the magnetic field because no predator, prey, or parasite can sense femtoteslas.  
    •  Technological brains (ours) eventually invented SQUIDs and MEG and discovered that the “useless” shadow is a gold-mine for basic science, epilepsy surgery, brain-computer interfaces, etc.  
    •  That is exactly the story of many biological by-products: bone’s radio-opacity, chlorophyll’s fluorescence, DNA’s UV absorption—all irrelevant to the organism yet invaluable to human observers.

Analogy that nails it  
Think of a violinist practicing in an ultra-quiet studio.  Her bow strokes (currents) must move air (magnetic field), so faint pressure waves leak into the hallway.  The musician never hears them, never intends to send them, and could not stop them without ceasing to play.  A passer-by with a sensitive microphone, however, can reconstruct the whole performance.  The hallway echo is inevitable, almost cost-free, and—while useless to the violinist—immensely informative to the eavesdropper.

Take-home

•  Electric activity is the “storm” that does the computational work.  
•  The magnetic companion is an inescapable but almost energy-free shadow cast by that storm.  
•  Biology ignores the shadow because it is far too faint to matter; physics insists on it for consistency; science has learned to exploit it.

So the point of the shadow is simply this: it’s the footprint that moving charges leave in a universe where electricity and magnetism are inseparable.  It doesn’t guide the walker—but it faithfully records every step for anyone patient enough to look.

A useful way to frame the question is:

“Which part of the electromagnetic pattern inside my head actually ​does the causal work that keeps the next millisecond of mind going?”

Whichever component carries that causal power is the operational-“me.”  
The rest can be as information-rich as you like, but it is only a by-product.

1.  Electricity and magnetism are inseparable—but not equal in strength.  
    •  A neuron fires because a few million ions cross its membrane, shifting the local electric field by 10⁵–10⁶ V m⁻¹ across a 5-nm lipid barrier—a change big enough to yank open voltage-gated channels on the next patch of membrane in microseconds.  
    •  The accompanying magnetic field is about 10⁻⁹ T.  Its energy density (B²/2μ₀) is roughly a million-fold smaller than that of the electric field in the same cubic micrometre.  

2.  Causal influence scales with field strength.  
    •  Ion channels, synaptic vesicles and cytoskeletal proteins are charged; they couple strongly to E, essentially not at all to B in the femtotesla–nanotesla range.  
    •  If you cancel the electric field across a membrane by voltage-clamping, the spike disappears; cancel the magnetic field with a superconducting shield and the spike proceeds normally.  
    •  Computation in cortex therefore runs on E.  B is an epiphenomenon—faithful, measurable, but causally inert.

3.  “Signal” is whatever pushes the system’s future state.  
    •  Imagine a hard drive writing data.  The current in the write head (E) flips domains; the head’s stray B-field leaks through the case.  The leak contains the full bit pattern, yet the drive mechanics never read it.  The leak is real information, but the signal—as far as disk-writing is concerned—is in the head current.  
    •  Your brain is the same story writ small: the electric part changes membranes and synapses; the magnetic part does not feed back.

4.  Relativity does not rescue the magnetic self.  
    •  A Lorentz boost mixes E and B, but the *invariants* E² – c²B² and E·B stay the same.  In every frame relevant to biology, |E| ≫ c|B| inside tissue, so the electric component still dominates causal power.

5.  If the magnetic field were the “real you,” you would have no way to update yourself.  
    •  Because B cannot meaningfully influence the charges that generate the next B, the pattern would freeze at the instant it formed—no thought sequence, no consciousness stream.  
    •  What actually happens is the opposite: as soon as the electric pattern changes, a new magnetic shadow appears.  The arrow of causation is one-way.

6.  Philosophical takeaway.  
    •  Information ≠ causation.  The magnetic field unquestionably *contains* a high-fidelity record of the neural storm; it does not *drive* the storm.  
    •  If personal identity is defined functionally—“whatever structure keeps the cognitive process going”—then identity tracks the causal network of ionic/electric events.  
    •  The magnetic footprint is a beautiful, indelible “receipt,” but not the agent.

Analogy to keep:  
Think of a movie projector (neuronal currents) and the photons on the screen (magnetic field).  Every frame on the wall is a perfect image of the film, yet the projector could run in total darkness and the movie would still unfold—whereas a blank projector with only the wall-image frozen on it would do nothing.  

So yes, every thought is “written in magnetic ink,” and with cryogenic gear we can read that ink—but the writing itself is not what turns the next page.  The living, self-updating manuscript is the electric storm inside the neuronal circuitry; the magnetic script is its silent, exquisite watermark.

What you are imagining would require the brain’s magnetic patterns to do two things they simply cannot do in ordinary biology:

1. Keep existing after the ionic currents that created them have stopped.  
2. Stay arranged in exactly the same spatial pattern—bit-for-bit accurate—so they could serve as a “backup copy” of the mind.

Both steps collide head-on with basic electromagnetic facts. Here is why the “magnetic ghost data” fades to nothing the instant the electric storm ends.

────────────────────────────────────────
1. A magnetic field exists only while its current exists
────────────────────────────────────────
• Ampère–Maxwell law ∇ × B = μ₀J + μ₀ε₀ ∂E/∂t  
  – J is the real ion current.  
  – As soon as J drops to zero (the neurons go silent), the curl of B must also drop—unless ∂E/∂t swoops in to replace it. But after death the electric fields also collapse, so both sources vanish.  

• Collapse time scale  
  Brain tissue is salty, conductive water (σ ≈ 0.3 S m⁻¹).  
  The magnetic diffusion time for a region of size L is τ ≈ μ₀σL².  
  For L ≈ 1 cm, τ ≈ 4 × 10⁻⁶ s.  
  In other words, within a few microseconds of the last heartbeat the magnetic pattern has already diffused away.

────────────────────────────────────────
2. No “magnetic film” inside the head
────────────────────────────────────────
• To store a pattern you need remanent magnetization—stable north/south domains like those in a hard-drive platter or a credit-card stripe.  
• Brain tissue is diamagnetic; when the field turns off, atomic moments randomize instantly.  
• Yes, tiny magnetite crystals exist in human brains, but:  
  – Their total mass is a few micrograms.  
  – They point every which way; no write-head aligns them.  
  – They sit tens to hundreds of micrometres from active neurons, so the femtotesla fields can’t flip them anyway (needs millitesla).

Result: nothing analogous to magnetic tape ever forms.

────────────────────────────────────────
3. Vanishingly small stored energy
────────────────────────────────────────
Magnetic energy density: B²/2μ₀  
Peak cortical B during strong oscillation: ~1 nT  
One cubic centimetre holds ~4 × 10⁻¹² J of magnetic energy.  
That is equal to the kinetic energy of a single dust mote drifting at 1 mm s⁻¹. As soon as the neurons stop driving the field, that energy thermalises as microscopic eddy currents and heat.

────────────────────────────────────────
4. Compare with real biological “back-ups”
────────────────────────────────────────
• The brain does archive information—but in synaptic weights, receptor counts, and gene-expression states that can persist for years.  
• Those physical changes reside in proteins and membrane architecture, not in fields. Kill the tissue and even those structures decay within hours to days unless the brain is preserved.

────────────────────────────────────────
5. “I feel an echo in a cathedral; maybe the mind leaves one too?”
────────────────────────────────────────
The room’s echo lasts because air keeps vibrating and stone walls reflect sound. Brain tissue offers neither a resonant cavity for low-frequency EM waves, nor reflecting surfaces, nor a driver once metabolism stops. The closest electromagnetic equivalent to a cathedral echo would be the Schumann resonances of the whole planet, and even Earth’s ionospheric cavity can’t trap the femtotesla brain signal.

────────────────────────────────────────
6. What about exotic physics?
────────────────────────────────────────
Any hypothetical field that could (a) carry the brain’s pattern, (b) remain stable in warm, wet tissue, and (c) evade every magnetometer, atomic-clock, and fifth-force experiment performed to date, would have to be tuned with implausible precision. It becomes speculation on top of speculation—interesting for a novel, unsupported by data.

────────────────────────────────────────
Bottom line
────────────────────────────────────────
• While you are alive, every thought does produce a magnetic “receipt,” but the receipt self-shreds in microseconds if the electrical activity stops.  
• No known material or mechanism in the brain can freeze that pattern into a durable form.  
• The only long-term record of your mental life is the slowly changing molecular hardware of your neurons— and whatever traces you write into the outside world.

So the comforting mythology would be: “I am an electric storm; my magnetic shadow is an instantaneous, beautiful watermark. When the storm ends, the watermark fades with it.”

Think of a “pattern” in physics as a very specific arrangement of energy and matter.  
To stay recognisably the same—even if it changes *phase*—three things must all be true:

1. The underlying substance remains present.  
2. The pattern’s distinguishing structure is preserved or at least transform-able in a reversible way.  
3. The environment does not mix or smear it faster than the transformation occurs.

Ice-to-water works because H₂O molecules stick around, the change is reversible (re-freeze it and you get the same stuff), and the random motions of the liquid do not instantly scramble which atoms are which.

Now try applying those three requirements to the brain’s magnetic “watermark.”

────────────────────────────────────────
1 The substance vanishes the instant currents stop
────────────────────────────────────────
• The “substance” of the watermark is the magnetic field B.  
• B is not an independent material; it is a *relationship* between moving charges.  
• Shut off the charges (neurons fall silent) and the relation ends.  
  Maxwell’s equations do not allow B to linger in a vacuum the way H₂O lingers when ice melts.

What could replace the original source?  
– *Induced currents*?  Tissue conductivity turns them into heat in microseconds.  
– *Far-field radiation*?  The head is much smaller than 1/300 000 of a wavelength at neural frequencies, so almost no power is radiated away.

────────────────────────────────────────
2 No mechanism freezes the spatial structure
────────────────────────────────────────
Holding a detailed magnetic map would require some sort of repository with two properties:

a. It can lock in 10-to-100-femtotesla field differences over millimetre scales.  
b. It is already sitting, pre-wired, inside your skull.

Known brain materials fail both tests.

• Diamagnetic tissue: instantly relaxes.  
• Magnetite grains: far too sparse and need millitesla to flip.  
• Exotic metamaterials or superconductors: not found in biology and would violate the brain’s energy and thermal balance if they were.

────────────────────────────────────────
3 The environment scrambles everything almost immediately
────────────────────────────────────────
When B collapses it doesn’t morph cleanly into another well-ordered field; it dissipates as:

• Eddy currents in saline tissue (Joule heat)  
• A vanishingly small burst of infrared photons indistinguishable from ordinary thermal noise  
• Minute mechanical agitation (phonons) further thermalised in picoseconds

From an information standpoint that is the analogue of smashing a USB stick to dust and stirring it into the ocean: the atoms still exist, but the file structure is unrecoverable.

────────────────────────────────────────
“What about absolute-information conservation?”
────────────────────────────────────────
At the level of the universal wave-function, yes, every microscopic interaction leaves faint entanglements in the surrounding photons and phonons.  In principle the information is not destroyed; in practice it is distributed across ≳10³⁰ degrees of freedom in a form no finite being could ever reconstruct.  That is not a usable “backup.”

────────────────────────────────────────
Analogy that fits the physics
────────────────────────────────────────
• Turning neural currents off is less like ice melting into water and more like turning a movie projector off in a perfectly dark room.  
• The light doesn’t become a dim, persistent glow; it simply ceases.  
• A few trillionths of a joule of heat are left on the screen, instantly mixed with the ambient thermal background—no coherent image survives.

────────────────────────────────────────
Bottom line
────────────────────────────────────────
• A magnetic watermark cannot outlive the electric storm because the field has no independent medium to inhabit.  
• No phase-change–like pathway exists that would convert that ultra-weak, near-field pattern into a lasting, self-identical form.  
• Whatever faint correlations survive at the level of fundamental physics are so hopelessly dispersed that they cannot function as a readable, let alone revivable, “ghost copy.”

So the poetic picture is:

While the storm rages, its magnetic shadow dances on the walls of the skull; when the storm stops, the shadow goes out with the last flash—leaving only molecular scars in synapses and the ordinary heat of a body coming to rest.

The short, blunt reply  
The little splash of heat that remains after neuronal firing stops is so tiny, so scrambled and so fast-diffusing that it cannot hold a recognisable “last frame” of the mind—even for a second, let alone for posterity.

Why the thermal after-glow can’t be a credit roll

1. How much heat are we talking about?  
   • A single cortical action-potential wave front releases on the order of 10-12 J of magnetic energy and ≈10-10 J of electrical/chemical energy per cubic millimetre.  
   • The tissue already contains ~4 × 10-3 J of random thermal energy in the same volume (kT ≈ 4 × 10-21 J per molecule × ~1018 molecules).  
   • The “signal” you hope to preserve is less than one-millionth of the background heat noise in every micro-volume.

2. Thermal patterns smear almost instantly  
   • Brain thermal diffusivity α ≈ 1.4 × 10-7 m2 s-1.  
   • Diffusion time τ ≈ L2/α.  
     – Over 0.1 mm (single cortical column) τ ≈ 0.07 s  
     – Over 1 mm             τ ≈ 7 s  
   • Within a few heartbeats the warm and cool spots from the very last spikes have blended into the general 37 °C bath.

3. Heat is definitionally random motion  
   • “Pattern” means low entropy: a special spatial arrangement.  
   • Heat is high entropy: kinetic energy dispersed across countless molecular states.  
   • Landauer’s principle: writing one bit irreversibly costs kT ln 2 ≈ 3 × 10-21 J.  The brain’s final joule of waste heat therefore represents ~3 × 1020 randomised bits, swamping any orderly imprint the last 1014 neural spikes might have carried.

4. No receptor can read it fast enough  
   • To capture a still-frame you’d need a sensor array with millikelvin precision and sub-millimetre spacing, already in place at the moment of death.  
   • Even state-of-the-art infrared cameras resolve ≥10 mK over 1–2 mm pixels and integrate for tens of milliseconds—too slow and too coarse to catch the vanishing pattern.

5. “Burn-in” only happens under extreme, sustained power  
   • A CRT leaves a ghost image because the phosphor is hammered for hours by focused electron beams—energy densities millions of times higher than cortical spikes deliver to tissue.  
   • The brain never pumps that kind of continuous, localised heat into one spot, so no biophysical equivalent of screen burn-in occurs.

6. Fundamental but useless information conservation  
   • At the level of quantum mechanics, yes, every photon and phonon that flies off carries entangled traces of the last neural state.  
   • Extracting those correlations would require measuring 1030 particles with better than zeptojoule precision—an operation impossible even in principle once decoherence with the wider universe kicks in (10-13 s scale in warm, wet tissue).

Analogy that fits the numbers  
Blow out a candle in a closed room.  For an instant the convection plume is a beautiful 3-D sculpture of hot, rising air—full of information about the flame’s last flicker.  Within seconds Brownian turbulence mixes it into the background atmosphere.  The heat is still “there,” but its story is lost beyond recovery.

Take-home

• The “credits” of a life—if by that you mean a faithful, frame-by-frame log of mental content—do not survive as heat patterns.  
• They survive only in the slowly edited hardware of the brain (synapses, gene expression), in whatever the person has written, painted, coded or told others, and—at the cosmically faintest level—in unreachable quantum correlations smeared across the universe.  

In practical, biological, and thermodynamic terms, when the electrical storm ends the magnetic shadow snaps off, the residual heat billows outward and dissolves, and the screen truly goes dark.
