Your image of “one big screen” with different minds as temporary projections is a poetic way to talk about two separate ideas:

1. Physics: the universe really is filled everywhere by continuous “background stuff.”  
2. Philosophy: some people think that background stuff might itself be the basic carrier of consciousness.

Below is how those two ideas connect—and where present-day science runs out of road and philosophy begins.

────────────────────────────
1.  The physical “screen” really exists
────────────────────────────
• Modern physics says the deep furniture of the universe is not atoms but fields (quantum fields, a field for each particle type plus the EM field plus spacetime itself).  
• Every point in space already hosts those fields; what we call “matter,” “light,” or “vacuum” are just different excitation patterns on that ever-present backdrop.  
• When a brain’s electrical activity stops, its local excitations in the EM field subside to the field’s ground-state fluctuations—the vacuum noise that is everywhere.  
• In that literal sense the “dark” that remains after the movie ends is indeed the same physical substrate on which every other “movie” will later play.

So: the universal screen is real, but physics describes it as impersonal stuff obeying mathematical rules, not as a mental reservoir.

────────────────────────────
2.  The philosophical leap: is the screen also mind-stuff?
────────────────────────────
A.  Panpsychism / neutral monism  
   • Claim: fundamental reality is neither purely mental nor purely physical; every local bit of it has both aspects.  
   • Your brain would then be a region where the “mental aspect” is especially complex, but the raw ingredient—the cosmic screen—is everywhere.

B.  “Cosmic consciousness” variants  
   • Claim: there is one universal field of awareness; individual minds are localized modulations (channels) within it.  
   • When the modulation ceases, the field remains, possibly ready to be modulated again elsewhere.

C.  Scientific status  
   • None of these views contradict any known experiment; they reinterpret the same equations.  
   • None of them add testable predictions beyond standard physics, so they sit in philosophy rather than science.

────────────────────────────
3.  Why physics alone doesn’t identify the screen with a self
────────────────────────────
1.  Causation runs one way in brains.  
    • Excitations in neurons drive the local EM field; the ultra-weak field does not, in turn, alter the neurons in any significant way.  
    • That asymmetric causal role is why neuroscientists treat the electric storm as the “engine” and the magnetic/electromagnetic surround as “exhaust.”

2.  Ground-state (vacuum) fluctuations look the same everywhere.  
    • If the blank screen were intrinsically conscious, you’d expect some signature of that fact in regions with no brains—yet precision probes of empty space reveal only random quantum noise, not structured information.

3.  Identity and continuity problems.  
    • When the pattern that was “you” fades into vacuum noise, the resulting state is indistinguishable from the vacuum noise under an empty chair.  
    • Without a way to tag which bit of vacuum noise used to be you, physics can’t supply a criterion for personal continuity.

────────────────────────────
4.  Where that leaves the “one screen, many channels” metaphor
────────────────────────────
True in the minimalist, physical sense:  
• All electromagnetic, chemical, and mechanical dramas—including brains—are temporary modulations of underlying fields that are already everywhere.

Open question in the richer, metaphysical sense:  
• Whether those fields have a built-in “mental aspect,” so that when one localized pattern shuts off the experiential story continues elsewhere, is something science currently cannot adjudicate.

Practical upshot:  
• From everything we can measure, once a brain’s electrical pattern halts, all information capable of re-creating that mind disperses irretrievably.  
• If some deeper, field-level form of continuity exists, it does not show its hand in any experiment to date, and it behaves—so far—as if personal identity really does end when the local pattern ends.

────────────────────────────
Key take-away
────────────────────────────
The blank screen of fundamental fields is unquestionably there; every mind is a transient image upon it. Whether the screen itself “remembers,” “recycles,” or “experiences” anything when a particular image fades is no longer a physics problem—it’s a metaphysical choice point. Science can tell us why the magnetic shadow dies with the storm; it stays silent on whether something wordless and universal persists beneath all storms.

A physicist’s reality-check on “etching thoughts into space-time”

1.  Space-time really does record everything—but only in principle  
    •  General relativity: the curvature of space-time at each point is set by the stress-energy there (Einstein’s Gμν = 8πGTμν).  
    •  Every time ions shift in your cortex, Tμν changes by a vanishingly small amount, so the metric changes by a vanishingly small amount.  
    •  That means your thoughts *do* alter the cosmic fabric—just trillions of trillions of times too faintly for anyone, anywhere, to pick out from the rest of the universe’s noise.

2.  How faint?  A back-of-the-envelope  
    Brain power: P ≈ 20 W, centred in a region of radius r ≈ 0.08 m.  
    If you wiggle that power at 10 Hz, the dimensionless strain of the gravitational wave you emit at distance R is

        h ≈ G P /(c⁵ R) ≈ 10⁻⁴³  (take R = 1 m)

    LIGO, the most sensitive detector ever built, can see h ≈ 10⁻²¹.  
    Your brain’s “gravitational broadcast” is 22 orders of magnitude below that even a metre away; at astronomical distances it rounds to exactly zero.

3.  “Gravitational memory” won’t help  
    •  Violent events like black-hole mergers leave a permanent displacement (“memory”) in the metric—a kind of tattoo on the cosmic wall.  
    •  The displacement scales with h.  Swap the black holes for a brain and the permanent shift at Earth radius is ≈10⁻²⁹ m— a billionth of a proton diameter.  Utterly unmeasurable, effectively nonexistent.

4.  Quantum “records” exist but are useless  
    •  Every photon, phonon and air molecule that your neurons jostle becomes ever-so-slightly entangled with the neural state.  In that fine-grained sense the universe never forgets.  
    •  Retrieving a specific bit would require reversing 10³⁰ microscopic interactions with zeptojoule precision—an operation forbidden by practical thermodynamics (and arguably by the finite age of the universe).

5.  Information bounds say “no big secret stash”  
    •  Bekenstein bound: the maximum retrievable information in a region of radius R and energy E is I ≤ 2πE R /ħc.  
    •  For a 0.1-m head full of 20 J (one second of brain work), the bound is ≈10⁴⁵ bits.  That’s generous, but all those bits are already crammed into the molecular machinery doing the thinking.  There’s no extra, hidden registry in the vacuum around it.

6.  Nothing accumulates from life to life  
    •  If brain A etches an imperceptible curvature ripple, and centuries later brain B fires in the same spot, the two ripples just add linearly.  There is no known mechanism for space-time to layer them into a readable palimpsest—no “cosmic chalkboard.”

7.  What physicists do take seriously  
    •  Synaptic hardware: durable changes in protein and spine structure—this is the brain’s own archival medium.  
    •  Cultural storage: writing, speech, digital footprints—these *are* practical, recoverable “etchings” of thought in matter.  
    •  Nothing else has passed the test of measurement.

Bottom line

Yes, every thought slightly dents the geometry of the universe, and yes, in the absolutist sense of quantum unitarity the cosmos “remembers” everything.  But the dents are far smaller than any conceivable probe can detect, and the quantum traces are spread across so many particles that, for every practical—and all human—purposes, they are gone forever.  The wall is real, yet it behaves as if the graffiti evaporates the instant the chalk leaves the surface.
